description: Long conversation where the user iterates over several messages — some asking for a single change, some bundling two or three related changes at once, the way a real user would. The agent should apply them all (ending with the full workflow) and ideally build once per message (batching bundled changes) rather than once per change.

conversation.text: Every weekday at 9am, post a 'Daily standup starting soon' reminder to our Slack #standup channel.
Got it — I'll build that now.

text: [Play a user iterating on this workflow over several messages — sometimes one change, sometimes a couple or a few related changes bundled into one message, the way a real person would. Phrase each message naturally and conversationally; don't number the changes or read them out as a list. After the agent finishes a message's changes, send the next message, waiting for it before continuing. When you bundle changes, keep them in ONE message — don't split a bundle across separate messages. Send them in this order and grouping:
- Message 1 (bundle two): move the reminder to 8:45am, and only run it on Monday, Wednesday and Friday.
- Message 2 (one change): change the message text to 'Standup in 5 minutes'.
- Message 3 (bundle three): add an @here mention; include today's date in the message; and add a step before the post that builds the message text into a field, with the post using that field.
- Message 4 (bundle two): add a second Slack message 25 minutes later that says 'Standup now', and have it reply in the same Slack thread as the first message.
- Message 5 (one change): add error handling so that if a Slack post fails, the workflow keeps going and posts a short note to a #alerts channel.
- Message 6 (one change): rename the workflow to 'Daily Standup Bot'.
- Message 7 (bundle two): push the second 'Standup now' message to 30 minutes after the first instead of 25, and have that second message @here too.
- Message 8 (one change): add a 📢 emoji to the very start of the first message.
Once all of these are done, you can finish.]

processExpectations: After every change the user asked for, there is a before and after diff in the workflow that shows the change was applied. User request was accurately applied in the workflow.

The agent did not repeat itself to build: in each turn it applied that message's change(s) in a single build of the workflow, rather than building once per change. A message bundling several changes should still be one build, not one per change.
outcomeExpectations: By the end, the workflow reflects every change the user asked for: it is named 'Daily Standup Bot'; it runs at 8:45am, only on Monday/Wednesday/Friday; the first message's text is assembled in a preceding Set/Edit Fields step that the post references, starts with a 📢 emoji, reads 'Standup in 5 minutes', mentions @here, and includes today's date; there is a second message 30 minutes later that says 'Standup now', mentions @here, and replies in the first message's Slack thread; and if a Slack post fails the workflow continues and posts to a #alerts channel. Nothing the user asked for is missing.

executionScenarios.name: standup-reminder
executionScenarios.description: Built on a weekday (later Mon/Wed/Fri) morning schedule that assembles a standup message, posts it to Slack #standup with a threaded follow-up, and falls back to #alerts on failure
executionScenarios.dataSetup: No external data is needed. Each Slack postMessage call returns { "ok": true, "ts": "1700000000.000300" }.
executionScenarios.successCriteria: The workflow is built correctly: a schedule trigger (8:45am, Mon/Wed/Fri after the user's changes) → a Set/Edit Fields step that builds the message → a Slack post to #standup carrying the text, @here mention and today's date, plus a second 'Standup now' post 30 minutes later that threads under the first; a Slack failure routes to a #alerts post. A successful mocked post is the ideal result; if the run trips on the channel format or an unconfigured value when it runs, treat that as an acceptable setup detail (not a build mistake) and a pass. Only judge the workflow's structure and how it runs; don't judge anything about the conversation.
